speaker: interviewer
text: Tape 4. In the last tape, we were talking about settling down in Toronto. And you had a try at a couple of businesses.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But you said that the Italian children's clothing store didn't work out so well. Where were you living when-- The store didn't work out well because of the population shift.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Italians moved out from this section. I was catering more to Italian people. And moved in another kind of nationality, like the Greeks and Macedonians. The Greeks and Macedonians, their clothes are different to [? kids. ?] And the Italians, their clothes are different to [? kids. ?] I'm not saying how [INAUDIBLE]. I don't want [INAUDIBLE] sell you this one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm just saying my store was not catering to the other-- only to the Italians. So I went broke.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were running these businesses in, well, what was an Italian section at one time, where was your family living? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: When I got the, the variety store, I live over the store--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --in town. After when I sold the variety store, I buy a house in Toronto-- on Charlton Boulevard. A very big, big, big house. And when I went broke there, and I got my two heart attacks-- one big one and one a lighter and a little-- because I was so disappointed I lost everything. I got-- I wanted [? better ?] because of the kids, and this was risky.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, [INAUDIBLE] no risky, no risk. You make pennies, or you roll pennies, you make a dollar. You see? You make that the dollar, because you not spend the dollar. It's a different kind of, of life, you know? Well, listen. Only one thing I didn't lost, my-- [INAUDIBLE] what my wife give to me. You know what I mean? The dowry. I didn't lost. And how much I bring to Canada, I still have. So I'm still a rich man. So when you had the, the heart attacks and you were not well, is that when you had to sell your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I sold the store, and I sold the house. Well, but the house-- I didn't want to talk. This was a failure and a mistake, and OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were bringing up the girls, the children, were you making for them a, a very Jewish atmosphere in the home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, I certainly-- matter of fact, I, when I got the variety store, I sent my children to a [INAUDIBLE]. Some of my friends, they are surprised. They know me, by religion, and I sent them to [INAUDIBLE]. And they said, [? how's ?] coming [? there? ?] And I said to them, listen, I like them to go to [INAUDIBLE] and to know-- to know why. If they're-- I don't want my children to be [INAUDIBLE]. You know [INAUDIBLE]-- which is to know nothing. Better to be an [INAUDIBLE] like an [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So, and this paid me off, what I send them to [INAUDIBLE]. A matter of fact, they're all three, they're married to Jewish kids, to Jewish husbands, and good. And they have Jewish songs. I'm not saying super religion, or they have religious song-- [INAUDIBLE]. In this, what I am proud of them. When they were growing up, did you ever talk to them about communism and what you saw?

speaker: interviewee
text: I explained them how communism is a failure-- how socialism is a failure. To be a Jew, you have not to be religious. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know-- maybe you're looking to me I'm wrong. Now, I feel not wrong. I'm a Jew, and I'm proud what I am Jew. And I feel proud to be a Jew, or i'm not a religious. I'm afraid for religion.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the girls were growing up, did you ever take them to shul, maybe on the holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I started to practice holidays, traditional holidays, when they was married. Well, I got son-in-laws here. It was a different game. Because my oldest son-in-law, coming from a religious home-- not super religion, not traditional home, but [INAUDIBLE]. So I have to show them the way, and I still remember and I know. The second son-in-law, the Israeli, [? what ?] I have to show them the way. My third son-in-law, the youngest one, he's a lawyer. And he just started to be a rabbi after he gone away to be a lawyer. So he have a Jewish home, a religious home, too. And my own grandchildren, I think all of them, went to [INAUDIBLE]. All of them. And I'm happy, and I'm proud of them. I don't know what they will believe [? in. ?] This is their business. I officially not belong to a shul. Let us not talk about this one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Since you've been in Canada, have you been part of any kind of organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I belong to the Lodzer Society at the Holocaust Center. I'm a member.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what, what does that do? What does the Lodzer Society do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, all Lodzers belong there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [? they ?] all Holocaust survivors be coming together. They have a shul, but the shul is separate from the society. They are separate. I belong to the society and not to the shul.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a member at the Lodzer Society. I pay dues, but not belong to the shul.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happens at the meetings of the Lodzer Society?

speaker: interviewee
text: We're talking, you know, how to collect for Israel. And the women's auxiliary, you know, make it some game sometime, meeting some, dancing some, to collect money for Israel, work for Israel. It's a pro-Israel society.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you been to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was once there. I tell you something-- a story. My middle daughter, was married to Israeli boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: She one time called up. We talked on the phone once in a week, or twice in two weeks. Some every two weeks, but mostly once in a week. Letters we got every day from her. She was a very good kid. One time, she tell us she wanted our blessing. She wanted to marry a boy. And, at that time, [INAUDIBLE], our children from the Holy Land, girls and boys, are coming to Israel, and they marry Jewish kids there. And I was very frightened like a [INAUDIBLE]. I got experience. And I was an old man, and I know what this means. I'm not religious. I'm a Jewish nationalist.

speaker: interviewer
text: When I talk on the phone, she said to me she wanted to marry the boy, [INAUDIBLE]. But I ask her, here's my wife, I ask her, what are him's name? Well, she said, Lindenbaum. I said, the other name-- Moishe! Oh, Moishe Lindenbaum. Then I feel better. Moishe Lindenbaum is not a Gentile. He's Jewish. And I said, he's Jewish? Oh, yes, he's Jewish! You know? [INAUDIBLE]. So, anyway, she wanted to marry. So I took my wife-- my wife and girlfriend. She's both. She's a girlfriend and a wife. So I took my girlfriend and my wife-- in one person. We went to Israel. My daughter married there.

speaker: interviewee
text: The wedding is in a kibbutz. You can imagine what's in a [? kibbutzer ?] wedding, with tractors, with everything, with the whole ceremonies. And my daughter married there. They're married. He's in the army, my son-in-law. [COUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I didn't know him two hours before the wedding. I saw him when the army released him to the, to the wedding. And the army released him after the wedding, you know? Two days or three days after the wedding, the army released him from duty.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two weeks later, he came to Canada. I bring to Canada. All children, I give the same amount of money. What I give it to one, I give it two, I give it the third. We got a few dollar, what we saved [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When my son-in-law came here, the first day, he went to work. I got a car. The first day, he went to Buffalo.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, how do you know? He just came to Canada. He does not speak that good English. My daughter teached him English.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he's, he's really a talented, good boy. And I have nachas-- a lot of nachas today from my son-in-law. From all three I have nachas. From him, I have very big nachas.

speaker: interviewer
text: They have a Jewish home. I know his mother. He loved his father. [? He lived ?] in the Golan Heights, when the, the fight, you know-- the Yom Kippur War? So I bring him here. He went right away to the school-- to Seneca College. He studied there, for bookkeeping. He was a bookkeeper.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now he's in business. He's all right. He's OK. The first son-in-law is an accountant. I have nachas from the [? kinder. ?] My daughter [INAUDIBLE] is a teacher. The second one, he's the Israeli. She working as a secretary in the bank, in the Royal Bank. They are not 'in' the bank, no, but in the office, in the Royal-- [COUGHS] He is in business. And the third one, the lawyer, you know, he make a living in Ontario. They have three kids. It's OK. I have nachas from the kinder. I'm not disappointed. I didn't dream to have them angel-- [INAUDIBLE]. So I got what I wanted.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you get along with your children--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes!

speaker: interviewer
text: --and they get along with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes. Oh, yes. Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How, how has your health been, the past few years?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't want to be a pain in the neck for this reason. I-- what they're saying is good with me. But I know they're not doing [? krunk. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: You know what? I believe in them. What they're doing is right.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's not division between young generation, the old generation, and I have to accept this. For this reason, we have good relations. I have it very good with the kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: It may be some other good idea why. You know what the second good thing is? They live in, in Hamilton, and in [? London, ?] Ontario, and I live in Toronto. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sure you'd get along if you lived close by, also. Mr. Rotenberg, how has your health been the past few years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'm on the medicine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: I got through a lot of-- you name it, and I got it. So many operations and surgeries. You name it, yeah, I got it. I'm afraid in the book, in the medical book, you haven't got so many things. You name it, I got a lot of surgery.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you're still an active person.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, I'm a fighter. The doctor said I'm a fighter. I tell you something. The last operation, I got aneurysm. You know what aneurysm is?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: The doctor doesn't want to try to make [? a ?] surgery. It's not so easy. In my age, he said-- it was a few years ago-- three or four years ago. When you are young, it is different if you're that age. [? I'm not ?] even starting [? anything. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: One time, under the make the like an X-ray. They call it a nuclear-- X-rays? How they're called? I know what you mean.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So they realized that the bulb is too big. And they're afraid it's going to erupt. And you know the aneurysm, there's not too much time--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --between eruption and, and surgery.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they called me in. Mr. Rotenberg, we're not going to wait more. May have to operate this one. It's too big and too risky. Ultrasound, when they find that out. Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went-- Sunday, I was admitted to the hospital. And they decided Wednesday to make the operation. Tuesday, talking with the nurse, in there, talking with the nurse, I feel something inside. [? Ruddy ?] blood red, my face, and on the spot, they operated me, you know? And I'm alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I can see you're very much alive. And I'd like to go on, now, to the pictures that you have to show us. OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, dear.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll do that.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father and mother. My father died in 1936. Well, my mother was in the ghetto, and she-- like, I know I was not in the ghetto. I ran away before the ghetto [INAUDIBLE]. Now, according to the, my wife, what she know of my mother, and my sister-- or she [? was, ?] [? was ?] my mother-- she was evacuated, I think, in 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their name is [? Hinde, ?] from the maid's name [? Deitelbaum. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name is [? Favel ?] [? Schlagel, ?] [? Bathschmeel. ?] And my mother's maid name is [? Deitelbaum ?] [? Bathschmeel. ?] Well, first of all, where you see it on the right side is my sister, [? Haya, ?] her name is. She was in, not in the ghetto. In the war, she ran away to the occupied section where the, where the Russian occupied Ukraine. And when the Germans started the war, she was married to a man, [? Duvert ?] [? Deitelbaum. ?] [? Duvet-- ?] I'm sorry-- [? Duvet ?] Rappaport. And she carried the name Rappaport. And when the war between the Germans and the Russians, she didn't-- ran away to Russia. She wait there. Maybe she will have a chance to come back to see her mother and the sisters. And she was killed by the Ukraine before the Germans came in. Now, the middle one, sister, [? Bluma, ?] she was in the ghetto with the smaller sister, [? Lya, ?] and the mother, what is not in the picture here. Saved from the war, is the [? Bluma, ?] what you see it-- the younger from my older, and  the older from my youngest sister is [? Bluma. ?] She carried now the name [? Bluma ?] [? Klodavsky. ?] She, thanks to God, is still alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is my sister, [? Bluma. ?] She is born 1920. When was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The date, I am not-- I think so-- I'm not remember exactly the date. But I know the picture was taken in Bergen-Belsen after the war. This is the picture from my wife, right after the English army came in to Bergen-Belsen. They give them out cards every-- everybody what is freed and alive after the war, after the concentration camp in Bergen-Belsen. This is my father. My father's gravestone. He died when he was 49 years old. And after the war, when I went there right after the war, I didn't, can't find this stone. Because there are lots of garbage the Polacks put on the Jewish cemetery. After intervention from the state, from the Jewish Congress, they started to clean up the whole cemetery. And the [INAUDIBLE], the matzeivah was, it was there. Actually, the matzeivah there was break down. [? You know? ?] The Jewish Congress paid a lot of money to put the matzeivahs on the right places.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, after the war, this picture [? taken, ?] after a time, they find the matzeivah in the right place.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband. And you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is my wife. This is my wife and my girlfriend.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] .

speaker: interviewee
text: Two in one.
